content_markdown:
Diagram 1 shows the variation with position of the displacement of a standing wave formed on a string.

Diagram 2 shows the variation with position of the displacement of a travelling wave moving to the right along a string.

Points P, Q, R and S are points on the string.

What is the phase difference between P and Q and the phase difference between R and S?

diagram_metadata.description: Two graphs showing displacement vs position. Diagram 1 is a standing wave with points P and Q located in the same loop (between the same two nodes). Diagram 2 is a travelling wave with points R and S separated by a horizontal distance of one-quarter of a wavelength.

options.label: A
options.text: Phase difference between P and Q: 0; Phase difference between R and S: 0

options.label: B
options.text: Phase difference between P and Q: $\frac{\pi}{2}$; Phase difference between R and S: 0

options.label: C
options.text: Phase difference between P and Q: 0; Phase difference between R and S: $\frac{\pi}{2}$

options.label: D
options.text: Phase difference between P and Q: $\frac{\pi}{2}$; Phase difference between R and S: $\frac{\pi}{2}$

vibe_explanation: In a standing wave, all points between two adjacent nodes vibrate in phase, so the phase difference between P and Q is 0. In a travelling wave, the phase difference $\phi$ is given by $\phi = \frac{2\pi x}{\lambda}$. Since R and S are separated by a distance of $\frac{\lambda}{4}$ (two grid units where a full wavelength is eight units), the phase difference is $\frac{2\pi (\lambda/4)}{\lambda} = \frac{\pi}{2}$.
